summary: Jürgen Schmidhuber outlines his Formal Theory of Creativity, Fun, and Intrinsic Motivation, which posits that subjective experiences like art, science, music, and humor are driven by a fundamental desire for data compression. According to the theory, an agent experiences 'fun' or intrinsic reward when its internal model successfully learns to compress previously unknown but learnable patterns. This drive for 'compression progress' explains why humans and artificial agents continually seek out novel, interesting tasks once old ones become perfectly predictable or boring.

principles.statement: Fun and intrinsic reward are mathematically equivalent to learning progress.
principles.rationale: An agent consists of a data compressor and a reinforcement learner. When the compressor improves its ability to encode the agent's history, the saved computational resources (bits) translate directly into an intrinsic reward for the reinforcement learner.
principles.source_quote: The learning progress of (A) can be precisely measured and is the agent's fun: the intrinsic reward of (B).

principles.statement: Interestingness is the first derivative of subjective beauty or simplicity.
principles.rationale: Data is interesting not because it is inherently beautiful, but because the observer is actively improving their ability to compress it. Once the learning process is over and the data is fully compressed, it remains beautiful but becomes boring.
principles.source_quote: We define the time-dependent subjective interestingness or novelty or surprise... as the change or the first derivative of subjective simplicity or beauty

principles.statement: Science, art, music, and humor are all by-products of the search for novel, compressible patterns.
principles.rationale: Whether it is a physicist finding a simpler law to explain data, a listener predicting a melody, or a reader understanding a punchline, the underlying mechanism is the brain rewarding itself for compressing previously unknown but learnable data.
principles.source_quote: The interesting or aesthetically rewarding musical and other subsequences are precisely those with previously unknown yet learnable types of regularities, because they lead to compressor improvements.

frameworks.name: Architecture for a Creative System
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building artificial scientists, artists, or curious developmental robots that exhibit open-ended learning.
steps: Implement a predictor or compressor (e.g., an RNN) of the continually growing history of actions and sensory inputs to reflect what is currently known.
Implement a learning algorithm that continually improves the predictor or compressor by detecting novel spatio-temporal patterns.
Calculate intrinsic rewards by measuring the predictor's or compressor's improvements (the first derivative of compressibility) due to the learning algorithm.
Use a separate reward optimizer or reinforcement learner to translate those intrinsic rewards into action sequences expected to optimize future learning progress.
frameworks.source_quote: To build a creative system we need just a few crucial ingredients: (1) A predictor or compressor... (2) A learning algorithm... (3) Intrinsic rewards... (4) A separate reward optimizer...

mental_models.name: The Brain as a Data Compressor
mental_models.description: Views the subjective observer as an entity constantly trying to compress its history of actions and sensations to save computational resources (bits). When it encounters a novel pattern and successfully updates its internal model to compress it, the saved bits generate an intrinsic reward (joy/fun).
mental_models.example: In humor, a punchline initially causes sub-optimal data compression because it violates expectations. But when the brain's learning algorithm kicks in and discovers the non-random pattern relating the punchline to the setup, it saves bits of storage, generating the intrinsic reward of a laugh.

mental_models.name: The Scientist as a Compression Algorithm
mental_models.description: Views the history of physics and science as an incremental process of finding shorter programs (simple laws) to compute and compress observed data better than previous laws.
mental_models.example: Newton's law of gravity acts as a short piece of code that allows physicists to substantially compress observation sequences involving falling apples and other objects.

heuristics: Maximize expected fun by finding or creating data that is better compressible in some yet unknown but learnable way.
Boredom happens when predictions can't be further improved; creativity is creating new tasks to learn novel skills on top of what is already known.

signature_quotes.text: The learning progress of (A) can be precisely measured and is the agent's fun: the intrinsic reward of (B).
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the relationship between the predictive learning module and the reinforcement learning module in a creative agent.

signature_quotes.text: His intrinsic fun is the difference between how many resources (bits & time) he needs to encode the data before and after learning.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the mathematical definition of fun for a subjective observer.

signature_quotes.text: When not occupied with optimizing external reward, physicists are just following their compression progress drive!
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how the formal theory of creativity applies to the scientific method.

signature_quotes.text: The boring patterns are those that are either already perfectly known or arbitrary or random, or whose structure seems too hard to understand.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why certain musical pieces or data streams fail to generate aesthetic reward.

anti_patterns.description: Postulating an objective 'ideal ratio' between expected and unexpected information to explain aesthetic experience.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the subjective observer's prior knowledge and learning process. The value of an aesthetic experience isn't defined by the object itself, but by the algorithmic compression progress of the specific observer.

anti_patterns.description: Explaining humor solely through the element of surprise.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Surprise alone could just be random white noise, which yields zero learning progress and is 'no fun at all.' It lacks the essential concept of novel pattern detection measured by compression progress due to learning.
